original_hypothesis.title: AuctionDemo Has No Function to Update gencore Address Creating Permanent Bricking Risk
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: AuctionDemo's gencore address is set in the constructor and has no update function, unlike other contracts (MinterContract has updateCoreContract, randomizers have updateCoreContract). If NextGenCore is redeployed or upgraded (via addMinterContract pointing to new core), the AuctionDemo cannot be updated to match. claimAuction calls IERC721(gencore).safeTransferFrom and IERC721(gencore).ownerOf, which would reference the stale core contract. Any active auctions would become unresolvable, permanently locking bidder funds.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Unlike every other contract in the system, AuctionDemo lacks a core contract update function. This is a missing-functionality defect that creates a permanent dependency on the original gencore deployment.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Constructor sets gencore = _gencore; no updateCoreContract function exists; gencore used in claimAuction at line 112-113 for ownerOf and safeTransferFrom

preconditions: NextGenCore contract address needs to change (redeployment, upgrade)
Active auctions exist with bids at the time of core contract change

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Immutable gencore address in AuctionDemo while all other contracts have update functions for core references
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: System-level decision to change core contract
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent once core contract diverges from AuctionDemo's gencore

operational_questions: Is there a planned upgrade path for NextGenCore?
How many active auctions could be affected?
Can a new AuctionDemo be deployed to migrate active auctions?
validation_requirements: Verify no update function for gencore in AuctionDemo
Test claimAuction behavior with stale gencore address
Assess migration difficulty for active auctions

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: NextGenCore is redeployed for any reason
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: AuctionDemo still references old core contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: claimAuction fails because old core's ownerOf/safeTransferFrom reference stale state
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All bidder funds in active auctions permanently locked
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None needed - triggered by standard operational upgrade

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All active auctions bricked after core contract change
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No on-chain recovery; new AuctionDemo deployment required; existing bid ETH locked

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Core contract address changes across system
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Failed claimAuction transactions after system upgrades

validation_result.mechanism: missing update function for immutable dependency
validation_result.impact_summary: AuctionDemo lacks an updateCoreContract function present in all other system contracts, meaning if NextGenCore is redeployed, active auctions become unresolvable and bidder ETH is permanently locked with no recovery mechanism.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies that AuctionDemo.sol sets the gencore address in the constructor (line 39) and provides no function to update it afterward. Every other contract in the system that references the core contract includes an updateCoreContract function: MinterContract.sol has updateCoreContract, RandomizerVRF.sol has updateCoreContract, RandomizerRNG.sol has updateCoreContract, and RandomizerNXT.sol has updateCoreContract. AuctionDemo additionally lacks update functions for its minter and adminsContract references. The gencore address is used in claimAuction at lines 112-113 for IERC721(gencore).ownerOf(_tokenid) and IERC721(gencore).safeTransferFrom(). If the MinterContract is updated to point to a new NextGenCore (via its updateCoreContract), mintAndAuction would mint tokens on the new core, but AuctionDemo would still reference the old core. Calls to claimAuction for these new tokens would fail because the old core has no record of them. Furthermore, AuctionDemo has no emergencyWithdraw function, so bidder ETH sent via participateToAuction for such auctions would be permanently locked.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: AuctionDemo.sol constructor at lines 36-40 sets gencore = _gencore with no subsequent update mechanism anywhere in the contract. A full search of AuctionDemo.sol confirms no function modifies the gencore state variable after construction. In contrast, MinterContract.sol line ~380 has 'function updateCoreContract(address _gencore) public FunctionAdminRequired(this.updateCoreContract.selector) { gencore = INextGenCore(_gencore); }'. RandomizerVRF.sol, RandomizerRNG.sol, and RandomizerNXT.sol all have equivalent updateCoreContract functions.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The gencore address is set once in the constructor and never updatable. No updateCoreContract, updateMinterContract, or updateAdminContract functions exist in the contract, unlike every other contract in the system.
Uses the immutable gencore address for ownerOf and safeTransferFrom. If core contract changes, these calls will reference stale state, causing the function to revert and locking all bidder funds.

calling_sequence: 1. Admin calls MinterContract.updateCoreContract() to point minter to new NextGenCore
2. Admin calls MinterContract.mintAndAuction() which mints token on NEW core via gencore.airDropTokens()
3. Users call AuctionDemo.participateToAuction() sending ETH bids (uses minter reference which also points to new core for auction status/time)
4. Winner/admin calls AuctionDemo.claimAuction() which calls IERC721(gencore).ownerOf(_tokenid) on OLD core
5. ownerOf reverts because the token was minted on the NEW core, not the OLD core
6. claimAuction reverts, auctionClaim was set to true on line 106 before external calls but the whole tx reverts
7. Bidder ETH is permanently locked - no emergency withdrawal exists in AuctionDemo
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability path begins when the system's core contract is updated. MinterContract.updateCoreContract (MinterContract.sol:~380) changes the core reference for the minter. When mintAndAuction (MinterContract.sol:~276) creates a new auction token, it mints on the new core. AuctionDemo.participateToAuction (AuctionDemo.sol:57-60) accepts bids using minter.getAuctionEndTime and minter.getAuctionStatus which work correctly since the minter was updated. However, AuctionDemo.claimAuction (AuctionDemo.sol:104-120) calls IERC721(gencore).ownerOf(_tokenid) at line 112 where gencore still points to the OLD core contract. The token doesn't exist on the old core, causing a revert, and all bid ETH is stuck.

deployment_assumptions_checked: AuctionDemo is not deployed in fixturesDeployment.js test fixture, but the pattern of contract deployment confirms all other contracts receive proper address references
config_validation.deployment_evidence: The fixturesDeployment.js file deploys NextGenAdmins, NextGenCore, NextGenRandomizerNXT, and NextGenMinterContract but does not deploy AuctionDemo. This means the deployment script is silent on AuctionDemo configuration. However, the code itself clearly shows the defect.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario does not require a malicious attacker. It is triggered by standard operational procedures: (1) Admin decides to redeploy or update NextGenCore for any reason (bug fix, upgrade, etc.). (2) Admin updates MinterContract to point to new core using updateCoreContract. (3) New auctions are created via mintAndAuction, minting tokens on the new core. (4) Users bid on these auctions normally. (5) When claiming, claimAuction references the old core where tokens don't exist, permanently locking all bid ETH. There is no recovery: AuctionDemo has no emergencyWithdraw, no updateCoreContract, and no way for bidders to extract their funds after auction end time since cancelBid requires block.timestamp <= auctionEndTime.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM-HIGH severity. While the trigger requires admin action (core contract change), the consequence is permanent loss of user funds with no recovery path. The AuctionDemo contract holds bidder ETH and has no emergency withdrawal function. Active auctions become completely unresolvable. The defect is particularly severe because: (1) it's inconsistent with every other contract in the system, suggesting an oversight; (2) there's no fallback recovery mechanism; (3) it affects user funds directly.
recommended_mitigations: Add updateCoreContract function to AuctionDemo: function updateCoreContract(address _gencore) public { require(adminsContract.retrieveFunctionAdmin(msg.sender, this.updateCoreContract.selector) == true || adminsContract.retrieveGlobalAdmin(msg.sender) == true, 'Not allowed'); gencore = _gencore; }
Add updateMinterContract function to AuctionDemo for consistency
Add updateAdminContract function to AuctionDemo for consistency
Add an emergencyWithdraw function to AuctionDemo to allow recovery of stuck funds as a safety net
Consider adding a refund mechanism that allows bidders to reclaim funds from bricked auctions
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy full system (Admins, Core1, Minter, AuctionDemo pointing to Core1). 2. Create collection and set up auction via mintAndAuction. 3. Place bids via participateToAuction. 4. Deploy Core2, update Minter to point to Core2 via updateCoreContract. 5. Create new auction on Core2 via mintAndAuction. 6. Place bids on new auction. 7. Advance time past auction end. 8. Attempt claimAuction - observe revert because AuctionDemo.gencore points to Core1 but token is on Core2. 9. Verify no recovery mechanism exists for locked ETH.
uncertainties: If the old core contract is never deprecated and tokens remain valid on it, existing auctions for old-core tokens would still work. The issue only manifests for tokens minted on a new core after the minter is updated.
The practical likelihood of a core contract change depends on operational decisions not captured in the code.
The contract is named 'AuctionDemo' suggesting it may be intended as a demonstration rather than production code, but the defect remains real regardless.
additional_testing_needed: Test claimAuction behavior when gencore points to a contract that doesn't have the queried tokenId
Verify that cancelBid and cancelAllBids also become unusable after auction end time, confirming no recovery path
Test the full upgrade scenario end-to-end to confirm ETH is permanently locked
